narrative:
As a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, based on academic and personal factors from past students. The model estimated a 71.8% probability that you would fail the bar exam, which is high enough to trigger an automatic rejection of your admission to protect the school's outcomes and reputation. This decision hinged primarily on three key factors from your profile, ranked by their influence on the prediction.

The most influential was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, which is well below the typical student's 5.53 average—this lower performance stood out strongly as a signal pushing the model toward predicting bar failure, as it suggests challenges in sustaining strong grades later in your education. Next, your undergraduate GPA of 3.00, compared to the average of 3.21, worked somewhat in your favor by nudging the prediction slightly toward passing, showing your college record wasn't a complete drag. Your upper-middle family income also played a role, contributing toward the failure prediction, possibly because the model associates certain income brackets with other patterns in bar outcomes.

Compared to typical students, your LSAT score of 34 falls short of the 36.7 average, and while your full-time student status aligns with the vast majority (over 92%), it didn't outweigh the top concerns. These elements together painted a riskier picture than for applicants who pass muster. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT or bolstering your recent academic decile through additional coursework or a post-bac program, then reapply to schools with more flexible thresholds—many students in similar spots succeed by targeting those improvements head-on.
